method3.generated_article:
==Etymology==
The English name *Iceland* corresponds to the Icelandic *Ísland*, pronounced approximately “ees-land.”[2] The name is generally explained as a Norse name meaning “ice land,” although some alternative theories have linked it to a Celtic origin and to the word for “island.”[2][4] According to the medieval sources *Íslendingabók* and *Landnámabók*, several Norse explorers were associated with the naming of the country, including Naddodd, who is said to have called it *Snæland* (“Snow Land”), and Garðar Svavarsson, who called it *Garðarshólmur* (“Garðar’s Isle”).[2][4] The name *Ísland* is traditionally credited to Hrafna-Flóki Vilgerðarson, who reportedly gave the island its lasting name after observing ice there.[4] By the time of later historical records, *Ísland* had become the established name in regular use.[4]

==History==
Iceland was settled in the late 9th century during the Viking Age, traditionally dated to the arrival of Ingólfur Arnarson in 874, and its early population was largely Norse with some Celtic influence.[3][4] The island’s historical development has been closely intertwined with its geology, as eruptions, earthquakes, and geothermal activity have repeatedly shaped settlement patterns and everyday life.[1][3] Written accounts of volcanic activity begin in the 12th and 13th centuries, but they refer to earlier eruptions reaching back to the time of settlement, allowing historians and geologists to reconstruct Iceland’s eruption history with unusual precision.[2] Over the past 11 centuries, researchers have identified 205 historical volcanic events, including eruptions ranging widely in size and duration.[2] Among the most significant were the Eldgjá eruption of 934–940 and the Laki eruption of 1783–1784, which were the two largest volcanic events in Iceland’s recorded history and among the largest on Earth in the last 2,000 years.[2] Iceland has also experienced frequent earthquakes, though modern construction has reduced their destructive impact.[3] Natural forces have continued to be central to Icelandic history, with the country’s landscape, culture, and settlement patterns shaped by the interaction of volcanism, glaciation, and the North Atlantic environment.[2][4]

==Geography==
Iceland is an island nation in the North Atlantic Ocean, situated between Greenland and the United Kingdom and just south of the Arctic Circle.[2][5] Its coast is deeply indented in places by fjords and borders several seas, including the Greenland Sea, the North Atlantic Ocean, and the Denmark Strait.[2][5]

The island lies on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the Eurasian and North American plates diverge, giving it a highly active volcanic and geothermal landscape.[1][3][5] This tectonic setting has produced volcanoes, geysers, lava fields, hot springs, and other dramatic landforms across the country.[1][2][4]

Much of Iceland’s interior is sparsely inhabited and consists of volcanic desert, mountains, plateaus, and ice fields.[1][2] Glaciers cover roughly one-tenth of the country, with the largest ice caps located in the central highlands.[1][3][5] The surrounding lowlands, especially along the coast, contain most of the country’s usable land for pasture and cultivation.[1]

Iceland’s landscape is often described as one of contrasts, combining glaciers and volcanic terrain in a relatively small area.[2][4] The country is also notable for its geothermal resources, which are harnessed for energy and heating because of the heat available at shallow depths.[3][5]

==Politics==
Iceland is a constitutional republic and parliamentary democracy with a multi-party system.[3][4] The president is the head of state and is elected by direct popular vote for a four-year term, while executive power is exercised by the government.[3] Legislative power is shared between the president and the 63-member Althingi, one of the world’s oldest parliamentary assemblies.[3][4] Members of parliament are elected by secret ballot every four years, and any eligible voter except the president and Supreme Court judges may stand for election.[3]

Following parliamentary elections, the president عادة asks the leader of a political party to form a cabinet, usually beginning with the leader of the largest party.[3] If that attempt fails, another party leader is invited to try to form a government.[3] Cabinets remain in office until the next general election or until a new government is formed, and ministers sit in the Althingi, although only elected members may vote there.[3] A cabinet must maintain majority support in parliament, or at least avoid censure, in order to remain in office.[4]

The constitution adopted in 1944 established Iceland’s modern political system after independence.[4] Although the presidency is largely ceremonial, it remains a visible institution in public life, and recent presidential elections have attracted attention partly because candidates have campaigned as being above party politics.[2] In August 2024, Halla Tómasdóttir became president after defeating former prime minister Katrín Jakobsdóttir.[2] The prime minister heads the government; as of the latest available information, Bjarni Benediktsson held the office.[2]

Iceland’s politics are shaped by coalition government and a broad range of parties, reflecting the country’s multi-party system.[3][5] Political alignments have been significantly reshaped since the 2008 financial crisis, with new parties gaining prominence and coalition patterns changing.[1] Rural-urban and left-right divisions remain important political cleavages.[1] Despite generally strong democratic performance, the country has experienced declines in economic equality and freedom of expression in recent years, while public trust and corruption perceptions remain politically salient issues.[1]

==Economy==
Iceland has a small but advanced mixed economy that combines free-market principles with a broad welfare system.[1][2] The country’s economic structure is shaped by its limited domestic market and its location between North America and Europe, so trade and export-oriented sectors play an outsized role.[2]  

The economy has traditionally depended on fishing, which remains a major source of export earnings, alongside aluminum and ferrosilicon.[1] Tourism has become one of the main drivers of growth since 2010 and now accounts for a large share of export value, with the sector recovering strongly after the COVID-19 downturn.[1][2] Manufacturing also contributes notably, especially through aluminum processing, while marine products remain an important export category.[2]  

Iceland’s abundant geothermal and hydropower resources have supported foreign investment in aluminum smelting and attracted interest from high-tech firms seeking inexpensive renewable energy.[1] In recent years, the economy has also diversified into services, software production, and biotechnology.[1] Knowledge-intensive services, including ICT, finance, and health, make up a significant part of employment, reflecting the country’s growing service economy.[4]  

The economy experienced a sharp contraction in 2020 because of the pandemic, followed by a strong rebound in 2022 and 2023 before weakening again in 2024.[2] Inflation rose in the wake of the pandemic but has since moderated, while unemployment has remained relatively low and the labor market has relied in part on foreign workers for unskilled and semi-skilled jobs.[2] Small and medium-sized enterprises also contribute meaningfully to turnover, indicating a relatively decentralized business structure.[4]

==Demographics==
Iceland has a population of about 402,000 in 2026, with a reported annual increase of 1.02%.[1] The country’s population is relatively small on a global scale, ranking 178th worldwide.[1] In early 2024, the population was reported at just under 400,000, reflecting continued growth in recent years.[3][5]  

The population has grown substantially over the long term, rising from 50,358 in 1703 to 200,000 in 1968 and 300,000 in 2007.[5] This expansion has been shaped by both natural increase and, more recently, immigration, which became a major contributor after 2005.[5]  

Iceland’s age structure is comparatively mature, with a median age of 36.6 years in 2026.[1] By the early 21st century, the country was described as having low fertility, small family size, low mortality, and high life expectancy, placing it in the fourth stage of the demographic transition.[5]  

The population is slightly male-dominated, with males making up just over half of residents and females about 48% as of early 2024.[3] A very small share of the population identifies as non-binary or another gender.[3]  

Iceland has become more diverse in recent decades, with about 20% of the population having foreign backgrounds as of early 2024.[3] Poles are the largest immigrant group, accounting for about 6% of the total population.[3]  

Life expectancy in Iceland is among the indicators tracked in international health statistics, along with population growth and age distribution.[4] Demographic projections also show continued change in the population’s age structure between 2023 and 2050.[4]

==Culture==
Icelandic culture reflects a blend of state support for the arts, strong preservation of heritage, and lively contemporary creative activity.[1] The government supports the conservation of historical sites and cultural artefacts, and it operates museums and national cultural institutions to promote research and public understanding of the country’s past.[1]

The Ministry responsible for culture oversees the arts, cultural affairs, youth affairs, and sports, and it aims to create conditions in which artistic variety, creativity, and initiative can flourish.[1] State support for the arts is intended to make arts and culture accessible to all residents while also sustaining a favourable working environment for artists.[1] Public involvement in youth affairs and support for youth organisations are also part of the ministry’s remit.[1]

Iceland’s cultural life is closely linked to scholarship and education, particularly through the University of Iceland, which serves as a major center for scientific research and higher education.[2] The university’s expansion over time into fields such as philosophy, engineering, and social sciences helped shape modern intellectual life in the country.[2]

Folklore remains an important part of Icelandic cultural identity, with enduring stories about elves, trolls, sea monsters, ghosts, and other supernatural beings.[3][5] Such traditions are often presented as part of the country’s broader heritage and continue to attract public interest, especially in connection with Icelandic landscapes and seasonal customs.[3][5]

Traditional celebrations and food customs also play a role in Icelandic cultural life.[4] Special observances such as Bóndadagur, Konudagur, and Þorrablót are associated with feasting, storytelling, singing, and games.[4] Iceland is also popularly associated with a vibrant music and arts scene, as well as museums, galleries, and other cultural institutions in Reykjavík.[4]
